notes.note: The monument of L. Minucius Basilus stood on the Via Appia just outside Rome; the spot was notorious for highwaymen, so it serves Cicero as a landmark for where his courier was robbed.

notes.anchor: the business is to be brought down to an interregnum
notes.note: If no consuls could be elected, the auspices reverted to the patrician senators, who appointed a series of interreges for five-day terms to hold the elections; the device had been used repeatedly during the electoral paralysis of the late 50s BC.

notes.anchor: or removed or expelled, or, claiming to have been expelled, has fled to him
notes.note: Cicero foresees the exact pretext Caesar would in fact use: in January 49 BC the tribunes Antony and Q. Cassius, after their vetoes were overridden, fled to Caesar's camp, giving him the grievance of violated tribunician sacrosanctity.

notes.anchor: Pompey for certain is to be in Spain
notes.note: Pompey held the two Spanish provinces under the lex Pompeia of 52 BC but governed them through legates while remaining in Italy; were Caesar made consul, Pompey would withdraw to his armies in Spain, leaving Rome to his rival.

notes.anchor: granted not by the Senate but by yourself, by force and by faction
notes.note: Cicero, ventriloquizing the case against Caesar, denies the legitimacy of his command: the Gallic provinces were secured through the political combine of 60-59 BC and popular legislation rather than a free senatorial grant.
